news_summary: Physicists made breakthroughs in understanding time's emergence, discovered a potentially habitable alien planet with continuous daylight, and announced exciting citizen science opportunities for the 2026 eclipse.
news_summary_fr: Les physiciens ont fait des avancées majeures dans la compréhension de l'émergence du temps, ont découvert une planète extraterrestre potentiellement habitable où la lumière du jour est permanente, et ont annoncé des opportunités passionnantes de science citoyenne pour l'éclipse de 2026.
news_summary_es: Los físicos han logrado avances decisivos en la comprensión del origen del tiempo, han descubierto un planeta extraterrestre potencialmente habitable con luz diurna continua y han anunciado interesantes oportunidades de ciencia ciudadana para el eclipse de 2026.

articles.title: Physicists created a tiny universe where time emerged without a clock
articles.summary: What if time doesn't actually exist until something changes? Scientists at the University of Birmingham created a tiny "mini universe" using 24,000 ultracold atoms and showed that the flow of time can emerge naturally from changes inside a quantum system, without relying on any external clock.
articles.content:
A physicist at the University of Birmingham has created a laboratory "mini universe" that brings scientists a step closer to answering one of the biggest questions in physics: What is time?

In a study published in Physical Review Research, Professor Giovanni Barontini demonstrates that it is possible to measure the passage of time without relying on a clock. Instead, the experiment shows that a version of time can emerge naturally from the behavior of a quantum system itself.

Why Some Physicists Think Time May Not Be Fundamental

Several theories of modern physics suggest that time may not exist as a built in feature of the universe. One example is the Wheeler-DeWitt equation, which describes the universe as a single quantum state with no external clock. In this picture, particles display both wave like and particle like behavior, and the familiar flow of time must arise from relationships between different parts of the system rather than from an independent ticking clock.

To investigate this idea experimentally, Professor Barontini created a simplified quantum "universe" using a cloud of 24,000 ultracold atoms cooled to just a few billionths of a degree above absolute zero. The atoms were sealed inside an isolated system and separated by a thin barrier created with two laser beams of different frequencies. This produced two regions: an observed ("bright") region and an unobserved ("dark") region.

A Tiny Universe With Its Own Sense of Time

Inside this miniature universe, the bright region repeatedly expanded and contracted, resembling a simplified version of a Big Bang followed by a Big Crunch, a hypothetical event in which the expansion of the universe eventually reverses.

Because the system was completely isolated, researchers could reconstruct the sequence of events using only information from inside the mini universe itself, without referring to any outside laboratory clock.

The results showed that time could emerge from changes taking place within the quantum system rather than existing as an independent background that always moves forward.

How Entropy Created Time

The experiment revealed that "time" arose from changes in the disorder, or spread (entropy), of the atoms as they moved between the bright and dark regions. Aside from this movement, the system remained isolated from the outside world.

As the distribution of particles in the bright region increased or decreased, the system effectively moved forward in time. When the particle distribution stopped changing, time itself effectively came to a halt.

Professor Barontini refers to this concept as "entropic time." In the experiment, this form of time:

Flows in one consistent direction, producing a clear "arrow of time"

Correctly orders events, even as the mini universe expands and contracts

Can speed up or slow down depending on how entropy is redistributed

Professor Barontini said: "In some theories of the universe, especially quantum gravity, time doesn't appear as a built-in feature. Yet in everyday life, time flows from past to future -- why is this so, when most basic laws of physics work the same way forwards and backwards?

"This study provides the first controlled experimental evidence that 'time' can be defined by changes within a system rather than as the external 'ticking clock' we think of as time. It offers new insight into the nature of time in quantum gravity that could be used to describe dynamics just as effectively as conventional time."

Testing Quantum Gravity in the Laboratory

The researchers also found that a version of the Schrödinger equation, the fundamental equation of quantum mechanics, can be expressed using entropic time. This means scientists can still predict how the "probability cloud" of a quantum system evolves over time even when time is defined by internal changes rather than an external clock.

The work tackles a long standing problem in physics. If certain theories are correct and the universe has no built in clock, how can events be placed in the correct order? The experiment suggests that the answer may lie in the system's own internal evolution.

Professor Barontini showed that the miniature universe follows the standard laws of quantum mechanics while allowing ideas about the nature of time, which are normally confined to theories describing the entire universe, to be tested under controlled laboratory conditions.

Toward Experiments on the Big Bang and Black Holes

The mini universe provides a valuable experimental platform for testing ideas in quantum cosmology and quantum gravity. Instead of relying only on mathematical models, scientists may now be able to investigate concepts related to the early universe through laboratory experiments.

The team says the same approach could eventually be expanded to more complex quantum systems, opening the door to experiments that explore the physics of the Big Bang, the "Big Crunch," simulated black holes, and competing theories about how time itself emerges.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough where physicists experimentally demonstrated that time can emerge naturally within a quantum system without an external clock. This advances fundamental understanding in physics and quantum gravity, with broad implications for cosmology and the nature of the universe. The work offers a new experimental platform for exploring big questions about the universe's origin and behavior, representing a meaningful and inspiring scientific achievement.
articles.personality_title: Scientists create tiny universe where time appears without a clock
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have long wondered what time really is and whether it exists on its own or comes from something else. Some theories say time might not be a basic part of the universe but something that appears from how things change inside it.

**What happened** – Researchers at the University of Birmingham made a tiny “mini universe” using 24,000 ultracold atoms trapped in a special container. This mini universe showed changes that acted like a small version of the universe expanding and shrinking. They found that time seemed to flow naturally from how the atoms moved and changed, without needing any outside clock to measure it.

**Impact** – This experiment is important because it proves that time can come from the system itself, not from a separate clock ticking away. It helps answer a big question in physics about how time works, especially in the strange world of quantum physics where usual rules don’t always apply. The study also shows that time’s direction is linked to how disorder (called entropy) changes inside the system.

**What's next step** – Scientists hope to use this mini universe to test other big ideas about the universe, like how the Big Bang or black holes work. They plan to try more complex quantum systems to better understand how time and space behave in extreme conditions.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Researchers created a tiny universe where time naturally emerges from changes inside, showing time doesn’t need an outside clock to exist.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques créent un mini-univers où le temps apparaît sans horloge
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques se demandent depuis longtemps ce qu’est vraiment le temps et s’il existe en soi ou s’il vient de quelque chose d’autre. Certaines théories disent que le temps n’est peut-être pas une partie de base de l’univers, mais quelque chose qui apparaît à partir des changements à l’intérieur.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université de Birmingham ont créé un « mini-univers » avec 24 000 atomes ultrafroids enfermés dans un récipient spécial. Ce mini-univers a montré des changements ressemblant à une petite version de l’univers en expansion et contraction. Ils ont découvert que le temps semblait s’écouler naturellement à partir des mouvements et changements des atomes, sans besoin d’une horloge extérieure.

**Impact** – Cette expérience est importante car elle prouve que le temps peut venir du système lui-même, pas d’une horloge séparée. Elle aide à répondre à une grande question en physique sur le fonctionnement du temps, surtout dans le monde étrange de la physique quantique où les règles habituelles ne s’appliquent pas toujours. L’étude montre aussi que la direction du temps est liée à la façon dont le désordre (appelé entropie) change à l’intérieur du système.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques espèrent utiliser ce mini-univers pour tester d’autres grandes idées sur l’univers, comme le Big Bang ou les trous noirs. Ils veulent essayer des systèmes quantiques plus complexes pour mieux comprendre comment le temps et l’espace se comportent dans des conditions extrêmes.

**Phrase clé** – Des chercheurs ont créé un mini-univers où le temps émerge naturellement des changements internes, montrant que le temps n’a pas besoin d’une horloge extérieure pour exister.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos crean un mini universo donde el tiempo surge sin reloj
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos se han preguntado durante mucho tiempo qué es realmente el tiempo y si existe por sí mismo o surge de otra cosa. Algunas teorías dicen que el tiempo podría no ser una parte básica del universo, sino algo que aparece por los cambios dentro de él.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de Birmingham crearon un “mini universo” usando 24,000 átomos ultrafríos atrapados en un contenedor especial. Este mini universo mostró cambios parecidos a una pequeña versión del universo expandiéndose y contrayéndose. Descubrieron que el tiempo parecía fluir naturalmente a partir de cómo se movían y cambiaban los átomos, sin necesidad de un reloj externo.

**Impacto** – Este experimento es importante porque demuestra que el tiempo puede surgir del sistema mismo y no de un reloj separado. Ayuda a responder una gran pregunta en física sobre cómo funciona el tiempo, especialmente en el extraño mundo de la física cuántica donde las reglas normales no siempre aplican. El estudio también muestra que la dirección del tiempo está ligada a cómo cambia el desorden (llamado entropía) dentro del sistema.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos esperan usar este mini universo para probar otras grandes ideas sobre el universo, como el Big Bang o los agujeros negros. Planean probar sistemas cuánticos más complejos para entender mejor cómo se comportan el tiempo y el espacio en condiciones extremas.

**Frase clave** – Investigadores crearon un mini universo donde el tiempo emerge naturalmente de los cambios internos, mostrando que el tiempo no necesita un reloj externo para existir.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of a glowing, translucent miniature universe inside a softly illuminated glass sphere, showing two distinct regions separated by a thin shimmering barrier of intertwined light beams; one side bright and expanding like a gentle cosmic pulse, the other dark and contracting, with swirling clouds of tiny luminous atoms flowing between them, symbolizing the emergence of time from internal quantum changes, all rendered in warm, natural tones with a serene and hopeful atmosphere.

articles.title: This alien planet never has sunrise or sunset. It may support life
articles.summary: A planet with one side permanently roasting and the other frozen in endless darkness might still have a chance of supporting life. Researchers found that heat inside a tidally locked exoplanet could circulate in a stable, continuous loop, helping moderate temperatures in certain regions. Their laboratory model suggests these worlds may be more hospitable than previously thought, despite their extreme surface conditions.
articles.content:
LHS 3844b is an exoplanet just slightly larger than Earth that orbits the red dwarf star LHS 3884, located 48.5 light years from our solar system. Unlike Earth, it is tidally locked, meaning it rotates once on its axis in exactly the same amount of time it takes to orbit its star. As a result, one hemisphere experiences constant, blistering daylight while the other remains in permanent darkness so cold it approaches absolute zero (zero Kelvin).

At first glance, such an extreme environment seems completely inhospitable. Daytime temperatures can reach roughly 1,000 to 2,000 Kelvin, while the night side is so cold that particle motion effectively stops. Yet new research suggests these worlds may not be as hostile to life as they appear.

"Just looking at the extreme temperatures on the day and night sides -- like 1,000-2,000 Kelvin on the day side and absolute zero on the night side -- might lead one to conclude these exoplanets are too harsh for life. But," says Daisuke Noto, a postdoctoral researcher in Hugo Ulloa's Penn GEFLOW Lab at the University of Pennsylvania, "life might find a way."

In a study published in Nature Communications, Noto and collaborators from the Japan Agency for Marine-Earth Science and Technology and Hokkaido University found that "such exoplanets may be more tolerant of sustaining life as 'tidal locking' can contribute to maintaining moderate thermal environments locally by distributing heat flux laterally."

Why Tidally Locked Exoplanets Are So Common

The findings challenge a common assumption about planets that always show the same face to their stars. According to Noto, worlds with permanent day and night are actually much more common than planets like Earth, which experience a regular day and night cycle.

"Many celestial bodies like moons and planets that are very close to their parent stars are what we call tidally locked," he explains. "Meaning, as they spin around on their axes and orbit around their parents, those rates/frequencies match, leading to the phenomena like us only seeing one side of our moon."

This constant orientation creates a dramatic temperature contrast across the planet. Instead of focusing on surface conditions alone, the researchers wanted to understand what happens deep inside the planet, specifically within the mantle, the thick rocky layer between the crust and the core.

Recreating an Alien Planet in the Lab

Rather than relying only on computer simulations, the team built a physical laboratory model to mimic the interior of a tidally locked planet.

"Building an actual exoplanet in the lab wasn't in the budget," Noto jokes.

Instead, the researchers used a tabletop rectangular tank filled with viscous glycerol and tiny thermochromic liquid crystals that change color as temperatures shift. Similar experimental systems have long been used to study how heat moves through slow moving materials, making them useful stand ins for the rocky interiors of planets.

Unlike weather or ocean currents, which are strongly influenced by Earth's rotation and gravity, convection inside a rocky mantle is driven mainly by differences in temperature and density. To reproduce those conditions, the team installed four thermostats around the tank to heat and cool different regions, creating temperature gradients similar to those expected between the permanently illuminated side, the permanently dark side, the surface, and the deep interior of a tidally locked exoplanet.

A Planetary Heat Engine

The experiments revealed a remarkably stable pattern. Hot material consistently rose beneath the day side, flowed across the upper region, cooled as it reached the night side, then sank before returning through the lower mantle. The result was one continuous circulation loop that behaved like a steady planetary heartbeat.

"It's not chaotic like Earth's mantle," Noto says. "It's slow and steady. Predictable. Kind of boring -- but in a good way."

The researchers also observed occasional mushroom shaped plumes rising from the heated base of the tank. Unlike volcanic hotspots on Earth, such as those beneath Hawaii or Iceland, these plumes remained fixed in one location rather than drifting over time.

Measurements of heat transport, known as Nusselt numbers, were comparable to those seen for Earth's mantle. That finding suggests some tidally locked exoplanets could maintain localized geothermal environments that provide conditions favorable for life, particularly in more temperate mid latitudes.

What This Could Mean for Alien Life

The steady circulation pattern may influence more than just surface temperatures. Noto believes it could also affect the movement of a planet's liquid core, potentially generating magnetic fields that differ from Earth's familiar dipole field.

"That's something we couldn't test in this experiment," he says, "but it's an exciting direction for future work."

Looking Beyond Other Worlds

Noto and Ulloa are continuing to develop similar laboratory models to investigate a wide range of geophysical processes. Earlier research from the Penn GEFLOW Lab explored how heat and mass move through confined spaces, providing new insight into the role of fluids in hydrothermal systems.

"We are planning on further extending the experimental methods to delve deeper into different systems on our planet in different contexts, the possibilities are, quite literally, out of this world," says Noto.

Daisuke Noto is a postdoctoral researcher in the School of Arts & Sciences at the University of Pennsylvania.

Hugo Ulloa is an assistant professor in the Department of Earth and Environmental Science in Penn Arts & Sciences.

Other authors include Takehiro Miyagoshi and Takatoshi Yanagisawa of the Japan Agency for Marine-Earth Science and Technology; and Tomomi Terada and Yuji Tasaka of Hokkaido University.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery about tidally locked exoplanets potentially supporting life due to stable internal heat circulation, challenging previous assumptions and expanding the scope of habitable worlds. The research has broad implications for astrobiology and planetary science, providing detailed experimental evidence and advancing understanding of planetary environments beyond our solar system.
articles.personality_title: New study shows some alien planets could support life despite extreme conditions
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Some planets outside our solar system, called exoplanets, can be tidally locked. This means one side always faces their star, staying very hot, while the other side remains in freezing darkness. Scientists have wondered if life could ever survive on such planets because of these harsh conditions.

**What happened** – Researchers from the University of Pennsylvania and Japanese institutions created a laboratory model to study how heat moves inside a tidally locked exoplanet called LHS 3844b. This planet is a bit bigger than Earth and orbits a red dwarf star 48.5 light years away. Using a tank filled with special liquids that change color with temperature, they simulated how heat flows inside the planet’s rocky layer. They discovered a steady circulation of heat that moves from the hot side to the cold side and back again.

**Impact** – This steady heat flow could keep some areas of the planet at moderate temperatures, making parts of it less hostile than scientists previously thought. It also suggests that tidally locked planets, which are very common in the universe, might have places where life could survive. The study challenges the idea that these worlds are too extreme for life and opens new paths for searching for habitable planets.

**What's next step** – The team plans to continue building similar experiments to explore other processes inside planets, like how magnetic fields might form on tidally locked worlds. Understanding these details could help scientists better predict which exoplanets might support life and guide future space observations.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Careful lab experiments show that tidally locked planets could have stable heat patterns that make some regions livable, changing how we think about where life might exist beyond Earth.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle étude montre que certaines planètes étrangères pourraient abriter la vie malgré des conditions extrêmes
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Certaines planètes en dehors de notre système solaire, appelées exoplanètes, sont verrouillées gravitationnellement. Cela signifie qu’un côté fait toujours face à leur étoile, restant très chaud, tandis que l’autre côté reste dans l’obscurité glaciale. Les scientifiques se demandent si la vie pourrait survivre sur ces planètes à cause de ces conditions difficiles.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université de Pennsylvanie et d’institutions japonaises ont créé un modèle en laboratoire pour étudier comment la chaleur circule à l’intérieur d’une exoplanète verrouillée appelée LHS 3844b. Cette planète est un peu plus grande que la Terre et orbite autour d’une étoile naine rouge à 48,5 années-lumière. En utilisant un réservoir rempli de liquides spéciaux changeant de couleur selon la température, ils ont simulé le mouvement de la chaleur dans la couche rocheuse de la planète. Ils ont découvert une circulation stable de la chaleur allant du côté chaud au côté froid, puis revenant.

**Impact** – Ce flux de chaleur stable pourrait maintenir certaines zones de la planète à des températures modérées, rendant certaines régions moins hostiles que ce que les scientifiques pensaient auparavant. Cela suggère aussi que les planètes verrouillées, très courantes dans l’univers, pourraient avoir des endroits où la vie pourrait exister. Cette étude remet en question l’idée que ces mondes sont trop extrêmes pour la vie et ouvre de nouvelles pistes pour la recherche d’exoplanètes habitables.

**Prochaine étape** – L’équipe prévoit de continuer à construire des expériences similaires pour explorer d’autres processus à l’intérieur des planètes, comme la formation possible de champs magnétiques sur les mondes verrouillés. Comprendre ces détails pourrait aider les scientifiques à mieux prédire quelles exoplanètes pourraient abriter la vie et guider les futures observations spatiales.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Des expériences en laboratoire montrent que les planètes verrouillées peuvent avoir des schémas de chaleur stables rendant certaines régions habitables, changeant notre vision des lieux où la vie pourrait exister au-delà de la Terre.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo estudio muestra que algunos planetas alienígenas podrían albergar vida a pesar de condiciones extremas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Algunos planetas fuera de nuestro sistema solar, llamados exoplanetas, pueden estar bloqueados por marea. Esto significa que un lado siempre está frente a su estrella, permaneciendo muy caliente, mientras que el otro lado está en oscuridad congelada. Los científicos se han preguntado si la vida podría sobrevivir en estos planetas por estas duras condiciones.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de Pensilvania y de instituciones japonesas crearon un modelo de laboratorio para estudiar cómo se mueve el calor dentro de un exoplaneta bloqueado por marea llamado LHS 3844b. Este planeta es un poco más grande que la Tierra y orbita una estrella enana roja a 48.5 años luz. Usando un tanque con líquidos especiales que cambian de color según la temperatura, simularon cómo circula el calor en la capa rocosa del planeta. Descubrieron una circulación estable de calor que va del lado caliente al lado frío y regresa.

**Impacto** – Este flujo estable de calor podría mantener algunas áreas del planeta a temperaturas moderadas, haciendo que algunas partes sean menos hostiles de lo que se pensaba. También sugiere que los planetas bloqueados por marea, muy comunes en el universo, podrían tener lugares donde la vida podría existir. El estudio desafía la idea de que estos mundos son demasiado extremos para la vida y abre nuevas formas de buscar planetas habitables.

**Próximo paso** – El equipo planea seguir construyendo experimentos similares para explorar otros procesos dentro de los planetas, como cómo podrían formarse campos magnéticos en mundos bloqueados por marea. Entender estos detalles podría ayudar a los científicos a predecir mejor cuáles exoplanetas podrían soportar vida y guiar futuras observaciones espaciales.

**Resumen en una frase** – Experimentos en laboratorio muestran que los planetas bloqueados por marea pueden tener patrones de calor estables que hacen algunas regiones habitables, cambiando cómo pensamos sobre dónde podría existir vida fuera de la Tierra.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a stylized alien planet split into two halves—one side glowing with intense red-orange light symbolizing perpetual daylight, the other cloaked in deep blue shadows representing eternal night—with a subtle swirling pattern beneath the surface illustrating steady, slow-moving heat currents connecting both hemispheres like a calm heartbeat.

articles.title: 2026 eclipse: 5 citizen science projects you can contribute to
articles.summary: During the August 2026 solar eclipse, scientists will be rushing to gather data on the sun, but even if you aren't a professional scientist, you can still help the research
articles.content:
During the August 2026 solar eclipse, scientists will be rushing to gather data on the sun, but even if you aren't a professional scientist, you can still help the research

There are several ways you can help scientists study this year’s eclipse Sirbouman/Alamy

During the total solar eclipse on 12 August, scientists from around the world will have their eyes – and scientific instruments – on the sun. But even if you aren’t a scientist, you can help in their investigations, both during the eclipse and year-round.

A total solar eclipse occurs when the sun and moon line up just right so that the moon blocks out the entire disc of the sun from the perspective of Earth. It is a huge cosmic coincidence that both the sun and the moon happen to be just the right size and at the right distance to give us such a spectacle, and a scientifically useful one at that.

On 12 August, a total eclipse will be visible over parts of Europe and the Arctic and Atlantic oceans, with a partial eclipse covering much of Europe, Canada, north-west Africa and parts of the US. An eclipse like this one is visible from somewhere on Earth just once every 18 months approximately, so during each eclipse, scientists rush out to gather as much data as they can. Here are some ways that you can help out:

Advertisement

1. Record shadow bands from the path of totality: In the moments before and after a total solar eclipse, strange phenomena called shadow bands billow across the ground. These bands are caused by the combination of Earth’s atmosphere and the hidden disc of the sun – it is similar to the effect that causes more distant stars to twinkle. If you are in the path of totality, all you have to do to see them is set out a white sheet or piece of cardboard perpendicular to the direction of the sun. To help out with this citizen science project, which aims to quantify how shadow bands differ based on altitude and distance from the centre of totality, just set up a camera to film the sheet.

2. Photograph the sun with your smartphone: The sun is very nearly spherical, but it isn’t perfect. One of the best ways to measure its shape with precision is to have lots of pictures taken from lots of different locations – that’s what SunSketcher does. It’s a smartphone app that you can set up and leave running as you enjoy watching the eclipse, and it will take carefully timed pictures to capture a phenomenon called Baily’s Beads, or the diamond ring effect. When the very last of the sun is about to be covered by the moon, the lunar landscape lets through tiny points of light, which shine along its edge. The same happens when the other side of the moon is just about to let the sun shine past it again, and these points of light are Baily’s Beads. When lots of photos of the beads, all precisely geolocated, are combined with a map of the lunar topography, that can give us an extraordinarily precise measurement of how far the disc of the sun is from a perfect circle.

3. Measure the darkening of the sky during the eclipse: Even if you are only going to see a partial eclipse, you can still help with scientific research. The Gaia4Sustainability project requires a little bit more equipment and set-up time, but once you have it sorted, you can leave it running all year round and keep collecting useful data. It consists of a small device with a bunch of sensors on it to measure the brightness of the sky and other meteorological factors, and the overarching goal of the programme is to measure light pollution so we can better understand its effects. But during the eclipse, the same sensors can be used to measure atmospheric changes caused by the blocking of sunlight, and the more different spots the team has data from, the more they will be able to learn about atmospheric dynamics during eclipses.

Free newsletter Sign up to Launchpad Bring the galaxy to your inbox every month, with the latest space news, launches and astronomical occurrences from New Scientist’s Leah Crane. Sign up to newsletter

4. Hunt for sun-grazing comets: As is the case for pretty much all astronomical events, a huge portion of the world won’t be able to see August’s total eclipse at all. Not to worry! There are still ways to get involved in solar science. In the Sungrazer project, you can download satellite pictures of the sun and look for moving objects on its outskirts. Some of the objects will be comets skimming past the sun, and once researchers know those comets are there, they can do more detailed research. A huge proportion of the known comets were discovered through Sungrazer. All it takes is a computer, an internet connection and some spare time.

5. Join a DEB observation team for next year: If you want to do something a bit more involved, the Dynamic Eclipse Broadcast (DEB) Initiative might be more up your street. It’s an scheme where teams receive training and some relatively basic equipment to observe eclipses across the path of totality, building up a huge repository of data that can then be used to study the evolution of the corona, the outermost layer of the sun’s atmosphere. Because of the training required, it is too late to join or create a DEB team for this year, but if you are going to be able to spot the 2027 eclipse that will sweep over northern Africa, you can sign up ahead of time.

Even during the eclipse, you shouldn’t look directly at the sun without a solar filter or eclipse glasses to protect your eyes.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant upcoming solar eclipse event and highlights multiple citizen science projects that enable the general public to contribute to meaningful scientific research. This promotes public engagement in science, broadens data collection for solar and atmospheric studies, and fosters a sense of participation in a rare astronomical event with tangible benefits for scientific understanding.
articles.personality_title: How you can help scientists during the 2026 solar eclipse
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – On August 12, 2026, a total solar eclipse will be visible across parts of Europe and the Arctic. This rare event happens when the moon completely blocks the sun for a short time, giving scientists a chance to study the sun and its surroundings in unique ways.

**What happened** – Scientists have planned several projects that anyone can join to help study the eclipse. For example, people in the path of total darkness can film shadow bands—wavy light patterns on the ground. Others can use smartphone apps to take special photos of the sun’s edge, or set up devices to measure how much the sky darkens. Even if you can’t see the eclipse, you can help by looking at satellite images to find comets near the sun. There is also a program called the Dynamic Eclipse Broadcast where volunteers get trained to collect data during eclipses.

**Impact** – These projects allow many people, not just professional scientists, to contribute important information. This helps researchers gather data from many locations at once, improving our understanding of the sun’s shape, the atmosphere’s behavior, and space objects like comets. It also makes the eclipse a shared experience that connects people with real science.

**What's next step** – The 2026 eclipse offers a chance to join these citizen science projects now. For those interested in more involved work, training for the 2027 eclipse in northern Africa is open. Scientists will use the collected data to learn more about the sun’s atmosphere and Earth's environment.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The 2026 solar eclipse invites everyone to take part in science by observing, recording, and discovering alongside researchers worldwide.
articles.personality_title_fr: Comment vous pouvez aider les scientifiques lors de l’éclipse solaire de 2026
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le 12 août 2026, une éclipse solaire totale sera visible sur une partie de l’Europe et de l’Arctique. Cet événement rare se produit lorsque la lune bloque complètement le soleil, offrant aux scientifiques une occasion unique d’étudier le soleil et son environnement.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Plusieurs projets scientifiques sont ouverts à tous pour aider à étudier l’éclipse. Par exemple, les personnes situées dans la zone d’obscurité totale peuvent filmer les bandes d’ombre – des motifs de lumière ondulants au sol. D’autres peuvent utiliser des applications pour smartphone afin de prendre des photos spéciales du bord du soleil ou installer des appareils pour mesurer l’assombrissement du ciel. Même ceux qui ne verront pas l’éclipse peuvent chercher des comètes près du soleil sur des images satellites. Un programme appelé Dynamic Eclipse Broadcast forme aussi des volontaires pour collecter des données pendant les éclipses.

**Impact** – Ces projets permettent à beaucoup de personnes, pas seulement aux scientifiques professionnels, de participer et d’apporter des données importantes. Cela aide les chercheurs à recueillir des informations de nombreux endroits en même temps, améliorant notre compréhension de la forme du soleil, du comportement de l’atmosphère et des objets spatiaux comme les comètes. Cela rend aussi l’éclipse une expérience partagée qui relie le public à la science.

**Prochaine étape** – L’éclipse de 2026 est une occasion de rejoindre ces projets de science citoyenne dès maintenant. Pour ceux qui souhaitent s’impliquer davantage, une formation est proposée pour l’éclipse de 2027 en Afrique du Nord. Les scientifiques utiliseront les données recueillies pour mieux comprendre l’atmosphère solaire et l’environnement terrestre.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – L’éclipse solaire de 2026 invite tout le monde à participer à la science en observant, enregistrant et découvrant aux côtés des chercheurs du monde entier.
articles.personality_title_es: Cómo puedes ayudar a los científicos durante el eclipse solar de 2026
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El 12 de agosto de 2026, un eclipse solar total será visible en partes de Europa y el Ártico. Este evento raro ocurre cuando la luna bloquea completamente el sol, dando a los científicos una oportunidad especial para estudiar el sol y su entorno.

**Qué pasó** – Se han organizado varios proyectos en los que cualquier persona puede participar para ayudar a estudiar el eclipse. Por ejemplo, quienes estén en la zona de oscuridad total pueden grabar bandas de sombra, que son patrones de luz ondulantes en el suelo. Otros pueden usar aplicaciones para tomar fotos especiales del borde del sol o instalar dispositivos para medir el oscurecimiento del cielo. Incluso si no puedes ver el eclipse, puedes ayudar buscando cometas cerca del sol en imágenes satelitales. También existe un programa llamado Dynamic Eclipse Broadcast para entrenar voluntarios a recolectar datos durante eclipses.

**Impacto** – Estos proyectos permiten que muchas personas, no solo científicos profesionales, contribuyan con datos importantes. Esto ayuda a los investigadores a reunir información desde muchos lugares al mismo tiempo, mejorando nuestro conocimiento sobre la forma del sol, el comportamiento de la atmósfera y objetos espaciales como los cometas. También convierte el eclipse en una experiencia compartida que conecta a la gente con la ciencia.

**Próximo paso** – El eclipse de 2026 es una oportunidad para unirse a estos proyectos de ciencia ciudadana ahora. Para quienes quieran participar más, hay entrenamiento disponible para el eclipse de 2027 en el norte de África. Los científicos usarán los datos recogidos para aprender más sobre la atmósfera del sol y el ambiente terrestre.

**Conclusión en una frase** – El eclipse solar de 2026 invita a todos a participar en la ciencia observando, registrando y descubriendo junto a investigadores de todo el mundo.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a glowing solar eclipse viewed over a serene landscape, with a gently billowing white sheet on the ground capturing subtle shadow bands, a softly illuminated smartphone silhouette nearby capturing the diamond ring effect, and a delicate array of glowing comet-like streaks orbiting the sun’s edge, all rendered in natural, muted earth tones.
